stint_ids: Hutchins, D. E___Cape of Good Hope___1888
Hutchins, D. E___Cape of Good Hope___1906
persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: the bio carries no honours (so the honour dimension can never corroborate), the staff lists print only initials 'D. E.', and the Kenya stint fails every deterministic colony/tenure/position check — a different D. E. Hutchins could in principle be the Kenya conservator. This fails. Forestry conservator is a rare, specialist office, and the three stints trace exactly the printed career: (1) Cape 1888-90 'Conservator' in 'Crown Forests and Plantations' matches 'conservator of forests, E. division, Cape, Jan., 1886'; (2) Cape 1906-07 'Conservator of Forests, Western Conservancy' matches 'conservator of forests, W. div., and consulting forest offr., Mar., 1892' — the position_sim FAIL compared the wrong clause (the 1907 E. Africa inspection) rather than the W. div. conservatorship; (3) the Kenya stint (1908-11, 'Conservator of Forests') matches 'ch. conservator of forests, E. Africa Prot., 1st June, 1907' — the East Africa Protectorate IS Kenya (renamed 1920; the graph normalizes the colony name), so the colony FAIL is purely a naming artifact, and the appointment date (June 1907) sits immediately before the first listing year (1908). The bio's edition run extends through 1911, exactly covering the Kenya listing years. A second D. E. Hutchins simultaneously pursuing the identical niche profession across the same two territories in the same years is not a realistic hypothesis; only 10 Hutchins officials exist in the whole corpus and no other forestry Hutchins is in evidence.

evidence_cited.stint_id: Hutchins, D. E___Cape of Good Hope___1888
evidence_cited.fact: conservator of forests, E. division, Cape, Jan., 1886

evidence_cited.stint_id: Hutchins, D. E___Cape of Good Hope___1906
evidence_cited.fact: conservator of forests, W. div., and consulting forest offr., Mar., 1892
ch. conservator of forests, E. Africa Prot., 1st June, 1907

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous D. E. Hutchins in colonial forestry, or a record showing the East Africa Protectorate chief conservatorship from 1907 was held by someone other than the man listed in the Kenya staff lists 1908-11.

notes: The Kenya deterministic FAILs are a colony-normalization artifact: the bio names the 'E. Africa Prot.' (the pre-1920 name of Kenya) and the compiled timeline mis-inherited 'Cape of Good Hope' for the 1907 East Africa clauses. The specialist office (forest conservator) plus exact division-level agreement (E. division, then Western Conservancy) anchors all three.
